title: Examples
description: Runnable programs demonstrating every major Elle feature

content.text: Elle ships with 20+ executable example programs. Each file is self-contained — run it, read the output, read the source. Start with `hello.lisp` and work down.

cargo run -- examples/hello.lisp         # start here
cargo run -- examples/basics.lisp        # then explore

rows: Smoke test — one line, proves the toolchain works
Type system tour: immediates, truthiness, arithmetic, the mutable/immutable split, bytes, boxes, equality
A gradebook built with defn/fn, closures, higher-order functions, composition, pipelines, variadic and mutual recursion
An expression evaluator: if, cond, case, when/unless, if-let/when-let, while, forever, block/break, full match patterns, -> / ->>
A contact book app exercising literal syntax, get/put, destructuring, each, threading, splice, string ops, grapheme clusters
Unpacking data: silent nil semantics, wildcards, & rest, nested patterns, struct/table by-key, match dispatch on struct tags
Error handling: error, try/catch, protect, defer, with, propagation, safe wrappers, validation
Cooperative sequences: coro/new, yield, lifecycle, Fibonacci generator, interleaving, nesting, yield* delegation
Macros and hygiene: defmacro, quasiquote/unquote, gensym, datum->syntax, syntax->datum
Parallel threads: spawn, join, closure capture across threads, parallel computation
Erlang-style actors: fiber-based scheduler, message passing with send/recv, links, trap-exit, crash propagation
Files, JSON, modules: slurp/spit, paths, directories, json-parse/json-serialize, import-file
Looking inside: clock primitives, time/elapsed, closure introspection, disbit/disjit, benchmarking
C interop: ffi/native, ffi/defbind, structs, variadic calls, callbacks (qsort)
User-defined signals, silence restrictions, early termination, progress reporting, logging, plugin sandboxing

content.text:
#!/usr/bin/env elle
# Simple hello world example
(print "Hello, World!")
(println)
From `functions.lisp` — a gradebook application that introduces Elle's function features:

# fn creates an anonymous function (a "lambda").
# (fn [params] body) — brackets delimit the parameter list.
(def average (fn [a b] (/ (+ a b) 2)))
(assert (= (average 80 90) 85) "fn: average")
(print "  (average 80 90) = ") (println (average 80 90))

# defn is shorthand for (def name (fn [params] body))
(defn letter-grade [score]
  (cond
    ((>= score 90) "A")
    ((>= score 80) "B")
    ((>= score 70) "C")
    ((>= score 60) "D")
    (true          "F")))

(assert (= (letter-grade 95) "A") "defn: A")
(assert (= (letter-grade 72) "C") "defn: C")
From `coroutines.lisp` — cooperative sequences with `coro/new`, `yield`, and lifecycle tracking:

# coro/new wraps a zero-arg function into a coroutine.
# coro/resume steps it forward; yield suspends and returns a value.
(def co (coro/new (fn [] (yield 42))))
(assert (coro? co) "coro/new returns a coroutine")
(assert (= (coro/status co) :new) "initial status is :new")

(def v (coro/resume co))
(print "  first resume: ") (println v)
(assert (= v 42) "first resume returns yielded value")
(assert (= (coro/status co) :paused) "status after yield is :paused")

(coro/resume co)
(assert (= (coro/status co) :dead) "status after body completes is :dead")
From `signals.lisp` — user-defined signals for flow control. Signals are cooperative interrupts that suspend execution and let the caller decide how to respond:

# Declare user-defined signal keywords
(signal :abort)    # early termination — caller catches and uses the value
(signal :progress) # progress update — caller can display or collect
(signal :log)      # log entry — caller decides whether to record it

# find-first scans a list and signals :abort the moment pred is satisfied.
# The rest of the list is never visited — no wasted work.
(defn find-first [pred xs]
  (each x in xs
    (when (pred x)
      (emit :abort x))))

# Drive from a fiber with a signal mask
(def f (fiber/new (fn [] (find-first even? [1 3 5 4 7])) |:abort|))
(fiber/resume f)
(assert (= (fiber/value f) 4) "found first even")
From `processes.lisp` — Erlang-style actors built on fibers, with message passing via `send`/`recv`, links, and crash propagation.
From `ffi.lisp` — calling C functions directly with automatic type marshalling:

# Load a shared library and bind a C function
(def libc (ffi/native nil))
(ffi/defbind libc puts [:string] :int)
(puts "hello from C")
